title: Example Downtime Activities
intendedUse: Structured data source for a web page or app that displays and processes downtime activities.

units.diceNotation: Standard tabletop notation, e.g. 1d6 means roll one six-sided die.

globalDmGuidance: The DM has the final say on which activities are available.
Available activities can depend on the area where the characters are located.
The DM can disallow magic item creation or buying if the location is too isolated or unsuitable.

activities.name: Buying a Magic Item
activities.summary: A character spends time and money looking for sellers of magic items, with no guarantee that the desired item is available.

costNotes: Spending more time and money increases the chance of finding a high-quality seller.
The monetary cost includes maintaining a wealthy lifestyle to impress potential business partners.

check.purpose: Determine the quality of seller found.

modifiers.condition: For every workweek beyond the first spent seeking a seller.
For every additional 100 gp spent on the search.
Optional DM modifier for low magic campaigns.
Optional DM modifier for high magic campaigns.

dmOptions: The DM decides whether a specific requested item is allowed in the game.
The DM may roll on the listed table or on any lower table associated with a lower check total.
The DM has final say on which items are for sale and their final price.
In low magic campaigns, the DM may double magic item costs.

rows.itemsAcquired: Roll 1d6 times on Magic Item Table A.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table B.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table C.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table D.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table E.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table F.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table G.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table H.
Roll 1d4 times on Magic Item Table I.

tables.title: Magic Item Price
notes: Consumables include items such as potions and scrolls.

chance.display: DM may roll or invent a complication.

rows.text: The item is a fake, planted by an enemy.
The item is stolen by the party's enemies.
The item is cursed by a god.
The item's original owner will kill to reclaim it; the party's enemies spread news of its sale.
The item is at the center of a dark prophecy.
The seller is murdered before the sale.
The seller is a devil looking to make a bargain.
The item is the key to freeing an evil entity.
A third party bids on the item, doubling its price.
The item is an enslaved, intelligent entity.
The item is tied to a cult.
The party's enemies spread rumors that the item is an artifact of evil.

activities.name: Carousing
activities.summary: A character spends a workweek socializing, drinking, and making contacts among a chosen social class.

accessNotes: A character with the noble background can mingle with the upper class.
Other characters need sufficient contacts, or may use a disguise kit and Deception to pass as a noble.

contactRules.contactDefinition: An NPC who shares a bond with the character and either owes a favor or bears a grudge.
contactRules.hostileContact: Works against the character by placing obstacles, but stops short of crime or violence.
contactRules.alliedContact: Can render aid, but not at the risk of their life.
contactRules.useLimit: A contact provides help once; the character must carouse again to get back into that NPC's good graces.
contactRules.unspecifiedAlliedContactMaximum: 1 + Charisma modifier, minimum 1. Specific named contacts do not count toward this limit.

examples: formal banquet or state dinner attendees

dmOptions: The DM may assign specific NPCs as contacts.
Alternatively, the player may later designate an NPC as a contact on the spot, provided the NPC matches the social class and the relationship is explained.
A mixed approach can provide both concrete NPCs and player flexibility.
The same options can be used for hostile contacts.

rows.result: Character has made a hostile contact.
Character has made no new contacts.
Character has made an allied contact.
Character has made two allied contacts.
Character has made three allied contacts.

rows.text: A pickpocket lifts 1d10 × 5 gp from you.
A bar brawl leaves you with a scar.
You have fuzzy memories of doing something very, very illegal, but can't remember exactly what.
You are banned from a tavern after some obnoxious behavior.
After a few drinks, you swore in the town square to pursue a dangerous quest.
Surprise! You're married.
Streaking naked through the streets seemed like a great idea at the time.
Everyone is calling you by some weird, embarrassing nickname, like Puddle Drinker or Bench Slayer, and no one will say why.
You accidentally insulted a guild master, and only a public apology will let you do business with the guild again.
You swore to complete some quest on behalf of a temple or a guild.
A social gaffe has made you the talk of the town.
A particularly obnoxious person has taken an intense romantic interest in you.
You have made a foe out of a local spellcaster.
You have been recruited to help run a local festival, play, or similar event.
You made a drunken toast that scandalized the locals.
You spent an additional 100 gp trying to impress people.
A pushy noble family wants to marry off one of their scions to you.
You tripped and fell during a dance, and people can't stop talking about it.
You have agreed to take on a noble's debts.
You have been challenged to a joust by a knight.
You have made a foe out of a local noble.
A boring noble insists you visit each day and listen to long, tedious theories of magic.
You have become the target of a variety of embarrassing rumors.
You spent an additional 500 gp trying to impress people.

activities.name: Crafting an Item
activities.summary: A character crafts nonmagical gear or, with additional requirements, a magic item.

resources.tools: Appropriate tools for the item being crafted.
resources.rawMaterialsCost: Half of the item's selling cost.
resources.equipmentRequired: Appropriate equipment, as judged by the DM.

notes: A character can complete multiple items in a workweek if their combined cost is 50 gp or lower.
Items costing more than 50 gp can be completed over longer periods if stored safely.

collaboration.requirements: Every collaborator needs the appropriate tool proficiency.
collaboration.dmGuidance: The DM decides how many characters can reasonably work on an item.

nonmagicalCrafting.result: If requirements are met, the process creates the desired item. The item can be sold at its listed price.

magicItemCrafting.summary: Creating a magic item is a long-term process involving formulae, rare materials, and often an adventure.
magicItemCrafting.consumableRule: Halve the listed cost and creation time for consumable items.
dmGuidance: Choose monsters or locations that thematically fit the item.
Facing a creature does not necessarily mean killing it; the creature might guard the needed location or resource.

examples.possibleRequirement: a piece of ancient stone touched by the god of magic and guarded by an androsphinx

tables.title: Magic Item Crafting Time and Cost

notes: Complications are most interesting when characters are working on a magic item.

rows.text: Rumors swirl that what you're working on is unstable and a threat to the community.
Your tools are stolen, forcing you to buy new ones.
A local wizard shows keen interest in your work and insists on observing you.
A powerful noble offers a hefty price for your work and is not interested in hearing no for an answer.
A dwarf clan accuses you of stealing its secret lore to fuel your work.
A competitor spreads rumors that your work is shoddy and prone to failure.

activities.name: Crime
activities.summary: A character gathers information, chooses a target difficulty, and attempts a crime for possible profit at risk of arrest.

outcomesBySuccesses.penalty: Pay a fine equal to the profit the crime would have earned and spend one week in jail per 25 gp of the fine.

outcomesBySuccesses.outcome: The heist fails, but the character escapes.

rows.target: Robbery of one of the richest figures in town

trigger: Roll or create a complication if the character succeeds on only one check.
If the character's rival is involved in crime or law enforcement, a complication occurs if the character succeeds on only two checks.

rows.text: A bounty equal to your earnings is offered for information about your crime.
An unknown person contacts you, threatening to reveal your crime if you don't render a service.
Your victim is financially ruined by your crime.
Someone who knows of your crime has been arrested on an unrelated matter.
Your loot is a single, easily identified item that you can't fence in this region.
You robbed someone who was under a local crime lord's protection, and who now wants revenge.
Your victim calls in a favor from a guard, doubling the efforts to solve the case.
Your victim asks one of your adventuring companions to solve the crime.

activities.name: Gambling
activities.summary: A character risks money in games of chance, making checks against random DCs determined by the quality of competition.

stake.notes: The DM may allow a higher maximum.

resolution.dcGeneration: Generate a separate DC for each of the three checks.
resolution.toolSubstitution: Proficiency with an appropriate gaming set can replace the relevant skill in any of the checks.

rows.value: Lose all the money you bet, and accrue a debt equal to that amount.
Lose half the money you bet.
Gain the amount you bet plus half again more.
Gain double the amount you bet.

rows.text: You are accused of cheating. You decide whether you actually did cheat or were framed.
The town guards raid the gambling hall and throw you in jail.
A noble in town loses badly to you and loudly vows to get revenge.
You won a sum from a low-ranking member of a thieves' guild, and the guild wants its money back.
A local crime boss insists you start frequenting the boss's gambling parlor and no others.
A high-stakes gambler comes to town and insists that you take part in a game.

activities.name: Pit Fighting
activities.summary: A character participates in organized nonlethal combat such as boxing or wrestling.

resolution.scope: Nonlethal organized fighting with predetermined matches.
resolution.deathMatchNote: For battle-to-the-death combat, use standard combat rules instead.
resolution.dcGeneration: Generate a separate DC for each of the three checks.

checks.specialBonus: Roll the character's largest Hit Die and add it as a bonus; this does not spend the die.

resolution.substitution: The character may replace one of these skill checks with an attack roll using one of the character's weapons.

rows.text: An opponent swears to take revenge on you.
A crime boss approaches you and offers to pay you to intentionally lose a few matches.
You defeat a popular local champion, drawing the crowd's ire.
You defeat a noble's servant, drawing the wrath of the noble's house.
You are accused of cheating. Whether the allegation is true or not, your reputation is tarnished.
You accidentally deliver a near-fatal wound to a foe.

activities.name: Relaxation
activities.summary: A character takes time off to rest, recover, and avoid engaging deeply with downtime systems.

benefits: Gain advantage on saving throws to recover from long-acting diseases and poisons while relaxing.
At the end of the week, end one effect that prevents regaining hit points, or restore one ability score reduced below normal.
limitations: The end-of-week benefit cannot be used if the harmful effect was caused by a spell or other magical effect with an ongoing duration.

complications.guidance: Relaxation rarely has complications. If desired, the DM can introduce an event connected to a rival.

activities.name: Religious Service
activities.summary: A character serves a temple through rites or proselytizing and may earn future assistance.

resources.accessRequirement: Access to a temple whose beliefs and ethos align with the character.

favorRules.definition: A promise of future assistance from a representative of the temple.
uses: Reduce the cost of cleric spellcasting by 50%.
Possible deity intervention such as an omen, vision, or minor miracle, at the DM's discretion.

rows.result: No effect. Your efforts fail to make a lasting impression.

rows.text: You have offended a priest through your words or actions.
Blasphemy is still blasphemy, even if you did it by accident.
A secret sect in the temple offers you membership.
Another temple tries to recruit you as a spy.
The temple elders implore you to take up a holy quest.
You accidentally discover that an important person in the temple is a fiend worshiper.

activities.name: Research
activities.summary: A character studies a specific person, place, monster, magic item, or other topic to learn useful lore.

minimumCost.purpose: materials, bribes, gifts, and other expenses

resources.accessRequirement: Usually access to a library or sage.

modifiers.condition: For each additional 50 gp spent beyond the initial 50 gp.
Access to a particularly well-stocked library or knowledgeable sages.

resolution.loreDefinition: Each piece of lore is one true statement about the researched person, place, or thing.
exampleLore: The password needed to enter a sealed dungeon level.
The spells commonly prepared by an order of wizards.
Elements of a monster or NPC's statistics or personality.
A hidden entrance, answer to a riddle, or nature of a guardian.
resolution.dmGuidance: The DM is the final arbiter of exactly what the character learns.

rows.outcome: You learn one piece of lore.
You learn two pieces of lore.
You learn three pieces of lore.

complications.riskTheme: False information, especially if a scholarly rival interferes.
rivalOptions: Bribe sages to give bad advice.
Steal key tomes needed to find the truth.

rows.text: You accidentally damage a rare book.
You offend a sage, who demands an extravagant gift.
If you had known that book was cursed, you never would have opened it.
A sage becomes obsessed with convincing you of a number of strange theories about reality.
Your actions cause you to be banned from a library until you make reparations.
You uncovered useful lore, but only by promising to complete a dangerous task in return.

activities.name: Scribing a Spell Scroll
activities.summary: A spellcaster transfers a known or prepared spell to a scroll by spending time, money, and required components.

requirements: The character must have proficiency in Arcana.
The character must provide any material components required for casting the spell.
The spell must be prepared or among the character's known spells.
resources.cantripRule: If the scribed spell is a cantrip, the version on the scroll works as if the caster were 1st level.

notes: Scribing is solitary; complications are more likely to involve preparation for the activity.

rows.text: You bought up the last of the rare ink used to craft scrolls, angering a wizard in town.
The priest of a temple of good accuses you of trafficking in dark magic.
A wizard eager to collect one of your spells in a book presses you to sell the scroll.
Due to a strange error in creating the scroll, it is instead a random spell of the same level.
The rare parchment you bought for your scroll has a barely visible map on it.
A thief attempts to break into your workroom.

activities.name: Selling a Magic Item
activities.summary: A character spends time and money finding a buyer for one magic item, then makes a check to determine the offer.

cost.purpose: spread word of the desired sale

resources.itemLimit: One magic item at a time.

check.purpose: Determine what kind of offer comes in.

resolution.sellerChoice: The character can choose not to sell, forfeiting the workweek and trying again later.
resolution.pricingMethod: Use base price by rarity, then apply offer percentage from the check result.

complications.riskTheme: Thieves, illegitimate buyers, and people attempting to undermine the sale.

rows.text: Your enemy secretly arranges to buy the item to use it against you.
A thieves' guild, alerted to the sale, attempts to steal your item.
A foe circulates rumors that your item is a fake.
A sorcerer claims your item as a birthright and demands you hand it over.
Your item's previous owner, or surviving allies of the owner, vow to retake the item by force.
The buyer is murdered before the sale is finalized.

activities.name: Training
activities.summary: A character learns a language or tool proficiency with enough time and an instructor.

resources.timeReduction: Reduce by a number of workweeks equal to the character's Intelligence modifier. An Intelligence penalty does not increase the time.

rows.text: Your instructor disappears, forcing you to spend one workweek finding a new one.
Your teacher instructs you in rare, archaic methods, which draw comments from others.
Your teacher is a spy sent to learn your plans.
Your teacher is a wanted criminal.
Your teacher is a cruel taskmaster.
Your teacher asks for help dealing with a threat.

activities.name: Work
activities.summary: A character finds temporary work, with quality and wages determined by an ability check.

rows.earnings: Comfortable lifestyle for the week + 25 gp

rows.text: A difficult customer or a fight with a coworker reduces the wages you earn by one category.
Your employer's financial difficulties result in your not being paid.
A coworker with ties to an important family in town takes a dislike to you.
Your employer is involved with a dark cult or a criminal enterprise.
A crime ring targets your business for extortion.
You gain a reputation for laziness, justified or not, giving you disadvantage on checks made for this downtime activity for the next six workweeks you devote to it.
